title: TPU v1 · Memory RTL for the 24 MiB Unified Buffer: Inference vs. SRAM-Macro Instantiation
oneLine: On-chip memories use two RTL approaches. Inference follows a supported coding template so synthesis recognizes RAM, which suits small register files. Instantiation connects a memory-compiler SRAM macro, which is required for large storage. A 24 MiB Unified Buffer cannot be built economically from generic flops; it must be partitioned across SRAM banks.

legend.name: Inference coding style
legend.desc: Declare an array and use a synthesis-recognized synchronous read/write template. The tool may map it to flops, distributed RAM, or a small SRAM. It is portable and self-contained, but deviating from the supported template can produce a huge bank of discrete flops.

legend.name: Macro instantiation
legend.desc: Instantiate an SRAM generated by the target process's memory compiler. RTL sees a black box with fixed clk/ce/we/addr/d/q ports; synthesis connects it, while .lib timing and .lef physical views define implementation. Large memories normally use this path.

legend.name: Small storage → inference
legend.desc: Tens to hundreds of entries—a small register file, state table, counter table, or shallow FIFO—can be inferred. Running a memory compiler for every tiny shape may cost more engineering effort than the acceptable flop or distributed-RAM implementation.

legend.name: Large storage → instantiate
legend.desc: Kilobyte- and especially megabyte-scale storage needs compiled SRAM macros. Dense 6T SRAM offers far better density, access timing, leakage, and dynamic power than flops, plus characterized models for implementation closure.

legend.name: TPU Unified Buffer → banked macros
legend.desc: TPU v1's 24 MiB Unified Buffer is an extreme case: a flop implementation would be infeasible in area and power. The practical RTL structure partitions the scratchpad into banks and instantiates a compiled SRAM macro per bank.

steps.cap: ① Inference: for a small memory, use a recognized array plus synchronous read/write template. Synthesis maps it to an appropriate RAM or flop structure; the source remains portable.
② Instantiation: for a large memory, instantiate a memory-compiler SRAM macro. RTL contains only a black box and port connections; vendor .lib/.lef views carry timing and physical behavior.

③ Select by capacity, port requirements, and target-process macro availability. Small and scattered storage favors inference; large regular storage favors compiled macros for density, timing, and power.
④ Apply the rule to TPU: a 24 MiB Unified Buffer is far beyond a sensible generic inference target and therefore follows the macro-instantiation path.
⑤ Partition the Unified Buffer into banks, instantiate a macro in each bank, and combine them for capacity and parallel bandwidth wide enough to feed the array.

before: Inventory every on-chip memory: record capacity, word width, ports, access bandwidth, latency, ECC, and clock domain, then decide inference versus instantiation per block before writing RTL.

Standardize inference templates: select supported synchronous read/write RAM forms and prohibit ad hoc asynchronous or mixed timing styles. Synthesis recognizes specific templates; unsupported forms may collapse into discrete flops.

Define the macro interface contract: confirm that a memory compiler exists for the target process and freeze port names, width, depth, latency, power modes, and the parameter-to-macro configuration mapping.

Define reset and width policy: SRAM arrays generally have no reset port and rely on initialization or valid-state tracking; surrounding controls follow the project reset policy. Freeze widths such as byte-granular 8-bit Unified Buffer data and 32-bit accumulator data where applicable.

Define synthesis and formal handling: design memory RTL contains no simulation-only delays or initialization. Treat instantiated macros as black boxes with the correct synthesis and formal models so internal implementation is not pulled into equivalence checks.
during: For inference, follow the template exactly: a synchronous-read RAM registers dout in always_ff. A combinational read or mixed read timing may prevent RAM recognition and infer flops. The template is a tool contract, not a stylistic suggestion.

For instantiation, treat the macro as a black box: connect clk/ce/we/addr/d/q and honor the characterized read latency. Align surrounding pipelines; do not assume data appears in the same cycle as the address.

Hide implementation choice behind one parameterized wrapper: expose one read/write interface while a conditional generate selects inference for supported small configurations and an appropriate macro for large ones. Process or capacity changes remain localized.

Bank large memories before instantiation: megabyte storage is not one giant macro. Divide it into implementable depth/width banks. Banking satisfies compiler size limits and provides parallel accesses or a wide aggregate port for the array.

Resolve port conflicts and read/write semantics in RTL: arbitrate a single-port SRAM that cannot read and write simultaneously, or distribute accesses across banks. Follow the macro's documented same-address read-during-write behavior and add forwarding only when required.

Keep the evidence boundary clear: inference, macro instantiation, and banking are standard RTL techniques. The disclosed 24 MiB size makes compiled banked SRAM the practical implementation, but the paper does not publish exact macro dimensions or bank count.

result.label: Small memory: inference example
result.value: reg [7:0] mem[0:DEPTH-1]; always_ff @(posedge clk) begin if(we) mem[wa]<=din; dout<=mem[ra]; end → inferred RAM/flops (illustrative)

result.label: Selection rule
result.value: Small/few-port → infer; large/regular → instantiate for density, timing, and power

result.label: Array reset
result.value: SRAM macro has no reset; use initialization / valid-state tracking

after: Lint and trial-synthesize the memory wrappers: confirm inferred storage maps to RAM rather than a sea of flops, and instantiated macros remain recognized black boxes. Inspect area and inference reports.

Verify timing and read/write behavior: cover synchronous-read latency, consecutive accesses, same-address read-during-write, bank boundaries, and surrounding pipeline alignment against the chosen macro or template specification.
Connect memory test hooks: production SRAM must be testable. Integrate MBIST or reserve the required test ports and confirm that test control does not alter functional timing.

Hand physical views to implementation: provide .lib and .lef for every macro. Confirm that bank placement fits within the ≤331 mm² die budget and that read paths close at 700 MHz; revise banking or macro configuration if not.

Hand off to verification and equivalence: verify memory function and configure formal equivalence to black-box the SRAM consistently or use its formal model, avoiding false mismatches inside the macro.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the 24 MiB Unified Buffer is disclosed; inference, macro instantiation, coding templates, and banking are standard RTL practice rather than published TPU source)
